## PAGE 00324: MODAF. AV-2 Integrated Dictionary

- page_id: `153990177`
- space_key: `UPDM2P2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/UPDM2P2024x/pages/153990177/MODAF.+AV-2+Integrated+Dictionary
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2017-07-04T11:07:14.337+02:00`
- ancestors: UPDM 2 Plugin Documentation > MODAF > MODAF All Views Viewpoint
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

**Description**

An AV-2 presents all the Elements used in an architecture as a standalone structure. An AV-2 presents all the Elements as a specialization hierarchy, provides a text definition for each one and references the source of the element. An AV-2 shows elements from the Ontology that have been used in the architecture and new elements that have been introduced by the architecture. Architectures often introduce new terms – usually because the architecture is covering new technology or business processes. The purpose of the AV-2 is to provide a local extension of the Ontology to explain the terms and abbreviations used in building the architecture.

**Implementation**

AV-2 can be represented:

- Using a report, which is automatically generated from all data.
- An AV-2 table. If you have AV-2 diagrams from previous versions, they will be migrated into the AV-1 diagram when loading the project with UPDM 17.0.2.

**Sample**

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### AV-2 Integrated Dictionary

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Fragment of AV-2 Integrated Dictionary report

#### PANEL: Related elements

Related elements

- Definition
- Alias
- Same As

#### PANEL: Related procedure

Related procedure

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Managing reports' space='UPDM2P2024x']

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=153990203 space=UPDM2P2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00325: MODAF. OV-1 High-Level Operational Concept Graphic

- page_id: `153990203`
- space_key: `UPDM2P2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/UPDM2P2024x/pages/153990203/MODAF.+OV-1+High-Level+Operational+Concept+Graphic
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2017-07-04T11:07:14.613+02:00`
- ancestors: UPDM 2 Plugin Documentation > MODAF > MODAF Operational Viewpoint
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

**Description**

The purpose of High-level Operational Concept Graphic is to provide a high-level graphical and textual description of operational concept (high level organizations, missions, geographic configuration, connectivity, etc) of what the architecture is supposed to do, and how it is supposed to do it. The OV-1, along with the corresponding AV-1 product is intended to serve as an executive summary of the architecture.

**Implementation**

OV-1 can be represented using:

- A link to an external document.
- An OV-1 diagram which is based on the UML Composite Structure diagram.
- An OV-1 Free Form diagram which is based on the UML Class diagram.
- A UML Composite Structure Diagram.

**Sample** 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### OV-1 High-Level Operational Concept Graphic

#### PANEL: Related elements

Related elements

- High-Level Operational Concept
- Concept Role
- Arbitrary Relationship

#### PANEL: Related procedures

Related procedures

- Creating OV-1 diagram

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=153990205 space=UPDM2P2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00326: MODAF. OV-2 Operational Node Internal Relationship Description

- page_id: `153990205`
- space_key: `UPDM2P2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/UPDM2P2024x/pages/153990205/MODAF.+OV-2+Operational+Node+Internal+Relationship+Description
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2017-07-04T11:07:14.635+02:00`
- ancestors: UPDM 2 Plugin Documentation > MODAF > MODAF Operational Viewpoint
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

**Implementation**

OV-2 can be represented using:

- An OV-2 diagram which is based on the UML Class diagram.
- An OV-2 diagram which is based on the UML Composite Structure diagram.
- A UML Class diagram.
- A UML Composite Structure diagram.
- A SysML Block Definition diagram.
- A SysML Internal Block diagram.

**Sample**

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### OV-2 Operational Node Internal Relationship Description

**Related views**

An OV-2 is highly related with an OV-5. Operational Nodes shown in the OV-2 are the performers of the Operational Activities modeled in the OV-5. OV-2 focuses on the Operational Nodes, with the activities being a secondary adornment. The OV-5, on the other hand, places first-order attention on operational activities and only second-order attention on Nodes, which can be shown as annotations or swim-lanes on the activities.

Information flows can be modeled either in the OV-2 or OV-5. In both cases they are highly associated and in general should be reused between these views.

The OV-2 displays the Capabilities required by Nodes from StV-2. That is an association between two abstraction levels of user requirements where the OV is more specific than the StV and a Node is more specific concept than a Capability.

The other important mapping is between OV-2 and SV-1. The specification Node and implementation Resource are subjects to map here. One OV-2 product can have several implementations in the SV-1.

#### PANEL: Related elements

Related elements

- Known Resource
- Needline
- Node Port
- Node Role
- Operational Exchange
- Service Channel
- Service
- Property
- Request

#### PANEL: Related procedures

Related procedures

- Creating OV-2 diagram
- Creating Operational Exchanges in OV-2 diagram

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html

OV-2 Operational Node Internal Relationship Description" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6>OV-2 Operational Node Internal Relationship Description</h6><p><strong>Related views</strong></p><p>An OV-2 is highly related with an OV-5. Operational Nodes shown in the OV-2 are the performers of the Operational Activities modeled in the OV-5. OV-2 focuses on the Operational Nodes, with the activities being a secondary adornment. The OV-5, on the other hand, places first-order attention on operational activities and only second-order attention on Nodes, which can be shown as annotations or swim-lanes on the activities.</p><p>Information flows can be modeled either in the OV-2 or OV-5. In both cases they are highly associated and in general should be reused between these views.</p><p>The OV-2 displays the Capabilities required by Nodes from StV-2. That is an association between two abstraction levels of user requirements where the OV is more specific than the StV and a Node is more specific concept than a Capability.</p><p>The other important mapping is between OV-2 and SV-1. The specification Node and implementation Resource are subjects to map here. One OV-2 product can have several implementations in the SV-1.</p></ac:layout-cell><ac:layout-cell><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="panel" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="62a36051-3a08-4516-b192-e507c7f0838b"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Related elements</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li>Known Resource</li><li>Needline</li><li>Node Port</li><li>Node Role</li><li>Operational Exchange</li><li>Service Channel</li><li>Service</li><li>Property</li><li>Request</li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="panel" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="f593d17f-1dd2-4d43-bd63-00047de445a7"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Related procedures</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li>Creating OV-2 diagram</li><li>Creating Operational Exchanges in OV-2 diagram</li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br /></p><p><br /><br /><br /><br /></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=153990207 space=UPDM2P2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00327: MODAF. OV-2 Operational Node Relationship Description

- page_id: `153990207`
- space_key: `UPDM2P2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/UPDM2P2024x/pages/153990207/MODAF.+OV-2+Operational+Node+Relationship+Description
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2017-07-04T11:07:14.659+02:00`
- ancestors: UPDM 2 Plugin Documentation > MODAF > MODAF Operational Viewpoint
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

**Description**

The Operational Node Connectivity Description is intended to track the need to exchange information from specific operational nodes (that play a key role in the architecture) to others. An OV-2 does not depict the connectivity between the nodes. MODAF modifies the OV-2 in two ways. First it recommends that an OV-2 diagram (now OV2a) shows the platforms or geographic locations at which operational nodes are deployed. Secondly it provides additional information (OV-2b) about each needline in the form of a requirements specification. There are now four types of needlines identified as follows:

1. InformationExchange
2. EnergyFlow
3. MaterielFlow
4. MovementOfPeople

In addition, MODAF permits service-oriented architectures. Instead of needlines between nodes, it is possible simply to show which services the nodes provide and consume. Finally, MODAF again permits known resources to be shown in an OV-2. However, this must be clearly shown as a KnownResource in an OV-2 model. LogicalArchitecture, which is the container class for all the nodes and KnownResources, is introduced.

**Implementation**

OV-2 can be represented using:

- An OV-2 diagram which is based on the UML Class diagram.
- An OV-2 diagram which is based on the UML Composite Structure diagram.
- A UML Class diagram.
- A UML Composite Structure diagram.
- A SysML Block Definition diagram.
- A SysML Internal Block diagram.

**Sample**

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### OV-2 Operational Node Relationship Description

**Related views**

An OV-2 is highly related with an OV-5. Operational Nodes shown in the OV-2 are the performers of the Operational Activities modeled in the OV-5. OV-2 focuses on the Operational Nodes, with the activities being a secondary adornment. The OV-5, on the other hand, places first-order attention on operational activities and only second-order attention on Nodes, which can be shown as annotations or swim-lanes on the activities.

Information flows can be modeled either in the OV-2 or OV-5. In both cases they are highly associated and in general should be reused between these views.

The OV-2 displays the Capabilities required by Nodes from StV-2. That is an association between two abstraction levels of user requirements where the OV is more specific than the StV and a Node is more specific concept than a Capability.

The other important mapping is between OV-2 and SV-1. The specification Node and implementation Resource are subjects to map here. One OV-2 product can have several implementations in the SV-1.

#### PANEL: Related elements

Related elements

- Node
- Logical Architecture
- Operational Exchange
- Exchange Element
- Node Port
- Node Role
- Needline
- Capability
- Exhibits
- Actual Location
- Location Type
- Energy
- Materiel
- Is Capable Of Performing
- Operational Activity
- Known Resource
- Resource Artifact
- Software
- Capability Configuration
- Organization
- Post
- Problem Domain
- Security Domain

#### PANEL: Related procedures

Related procedures

- Creating OV-2 diagram
- Creating Operational Exchanges in OV-2 diagram

#### PANEL: Related references

Related references

- Operational Exchange Creation Wizard
- Operational Exchange Manager Dialog

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:layout><ac:layout-section ac:type="two_right_sidebar"><ac:layout-cell><p><strong>Description</strong></p><p>The Operational Node Connectivity Description is intended to track the need to exchange information from specific operational nodes (that play a key role in the architecture) to others. An OV-2 does not depict the connectivity between the nodes. MODAF modifies the OV-2 in two ways. First it recommends that an OV-2 diagram (now OV2a) shows the platforms or geographic locations at which operational nodes are deployed. Secondly it provides additional information (OV-2b) about each needline in the form of a requirements specification. There are now four types of needlines identified as follows:</p><ol><li>InformationExchange</li><li>EnergyFlow</li><li>MaterielFlow</li><li>MovementOfPeople</li></ol><p>In addition, MODAF permits service-oriented architectures. Instead of needlines between nodes, it is possible simply to show which services the nodes provide and consume. Finally, MODAF again permits known resources to be shown in an OV-2. However, this must be clearly shown as a KnownResource in an OV-2 model. LogicalArchitecture, which is the container class for all the nodes and KnownResources, is introduced.</p><p><strong>Implementation</strong></p><p>OV-2 can be represented using:</p><ul><li>An OV-2 diagram which is based on the UML Class diagram.</li><li>An OV-2 diagram which is based on the UML Composite Structure diagram.</li><li>A UML Class diagram.</li><li>A UML Composite Structure diagram.</li><li>A SysML Block Definition diagram.</li><li>A SysML Internal Block diagram.</li></ul><p><strong>Sample</strong></p><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Ov_2.jpg"><ri:page ri:space-key="UPDM2P2024x" ri:content-title="MODAF. OV-2 Operational Node Relationship Description" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6>OV-2 Operational Node Relationship Description</h6><p><strong>Related views</strong></p><p>An OV-2 is highly related with an OV-5. Operational Nodes shown in the OV-2 are the performers of the Operational Activities modeled in the OV-5. OV-2 focuses on the Operational Nodes, with the activities being a secondary adornment. The OV-5, on the other hand, places first-order attention on operational activities and only second-order attention on Nodes, which can be shown as annotations or swim-lanes on the activities. </p><p>Information flows can be modeled either in the OV-2 or OV-5. In both cases they are highly associated and in general should be reused between these views.</p><p>The OV-2 displays the Capabilities required by Nodes from StV-2. That is an association between two abstraction levels of user requirements where the OV is more specific than the StV and a Node is more specific concept than a Capability.</p><p>The other important mapping is between OV-2 and SV-1. The specification Node and implementation Resource are subjects to map here. One OV-2 product can have several implementations in the SV-1.</p></ac:layout-cell><ac:layout-cell><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="panel" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="f33505b5-64f9-4313-b17d-5dc11c676a93"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Related elements</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li>Node</li><li>Logical Architecture</li><li>Operational Exchange</li><li>Exchange Element</li><li>Node Port</li><li>Node Role</li><li>Needline</li><li>Capability</li><li>Exhibits</li><li>Actual Location</li><li>Location Type</li><li>Energy</li><li>Materiel</li><li>Is Capable Of Performing</li><li>Operational Activity</li><li>Known Resource</li><li>Resource Artifact</li><li>Software</li><li>Capability Configuration</li><li>Organization</li><li>Post</li><li>Problem Domain</li><li>Security Domain</li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="panel" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="b6ff755d-588d-438c-839d-4affdf254603"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Related procedures</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li>Creating OV-2 diagram</li><li>Creating Operational Exchanges in OV-2 diagram</li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="panel" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="f46a0ac0-094b-4621-9b5d-8f4a1ff04bc8"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Related references</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li>Operational Exchange Creation Wizard</li><li>Operational Exchange Manager Dialog</li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br /></p><p><br /></p><p><br /><br /><br /><br /></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=153990209 space=UPDM2P2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00328: MODAF. OV-3 Operational Information Exchange Matrix

- page_id: `153990209`
- space_key: `UPDM2P2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/UPDM2P2024x/pages/153990209/MODAF.+OV-3+Operational+Information+Exchange+Matrix
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2017-07-04T11:07:14.683+02:00`
- ancestors: UPDM 2 Plugin Documentation > MODAF > MODAF Operational Viewpoint
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

**Description**

Information exchanges express the relationship across the three basic architecture data elements of an OV (operational activities, operational nodes, and information flow) with a focus on the specific aspects of the information flow and the information content.

The Information Exchanges of the OV-3 should remain at a high level of aggregation to represent actual information workflow products that are used at the operational nodes shown in the OV-2 (and not their subordinate operational nodes).

**Implementation**

OV-3 can be represented using:

- An OV-3 table.
- An OV-3 role-based table. A role-based table represents exchanges between Node Roles (node usages).
- An OV-3 spreadsheet report.

**Sample**

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### OV-3 Operational Information Exchange Matrix

**Related views**

An OV-3 is initially constructed from the information contained in the Operational Node Connectivity Description (OV-2).

#### PANEL: Related elements

Related elements

- Node
- Operational Exchange
- Exchange Element
- Energy
- Materiel
- Resource Artifact
- Post
- Software
- Capability Configuration
- Organization
- Operational Activity

#### PANEL: Related procedures

Related procedures

- Creating OV-3 table
- Displaying Custom Measurements

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=153990211 space=UPDM2P2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00329: MODAF. OV-4 Organizational Relationships Chart

- page_id: `153990211`
- space_key: `UPDM2P2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/UPDM2P2024x/pages/153990211/MODAF.+OV-4+Organizational+Relationships+Chart
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2017-07-04T11:07:14.707+02:00`
- ancestors: UPDM 2 Plugin Documentation > MODAF > MODAF Operational Viewpoint
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

**Description**

The Organizational Relationships Chart illustrates the command structure or relationships (as opposed to relationships with respect to a business process flow) among human roles, organizations, or organization types that are the key players in architecture. MODAF divides The OV-4 in two views: an OV-4 Typical and an OV-4 Actual. The former is exactly as the DoDAF OV-4, while the latter is a special form of the SV-1; where the resources are restricted to being organizational.

**Implementation**

OV-4 can be represented using:

- An OV-4 diagram which is based on the UML Class diagram.
- A UML Class diagram.
- A SysML Block Definition diagram.

**Sample**

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### OV-4 Organizational Relationships Chart

**Related views**

The organizations that are modeled using an OV-4 in the Operational Viewpoint may also appear in other views, for example, SV-1 (organizational constituents of a capability configuration), AcV-1 (actual organizations that own projects), and SV-5 (organizational resources performs functions).

#### PANEL: Related elements

Related elements

- Organization
- Post
- Person
- Command
- Competence
- Provides Competence
- Requires Competence
- Actual Organization
- Actual Post
- Actual Person
- Fills Post
- Actual Organization Relationship
- Actual Organization Role
- Operational Activity
- Owns Process

#### PANEL: Related procedures

Related procedures

- Creating OV-4 diagram
- Instantiating Structures

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:layout><ac:layout-section ac:type="two_right_sidebar"><ac:layout-cell><p><strong>Description</strong></p><p>The Organizational Relationships Chart illustrates the command structure or relationships (as opposed to relationships with respect to a business process flow) among human roles, organizations, or organization types that are the key players in architecture. MODAF divides The OV-4 in two views: an OV-4 Typical and an OV-4 Actual. The former is exactly as the DoDAF OV-4, while the latter is a special form of the SV-1; where the resources are restricted to being organizational.</p><p><strong>Implementation</strong></p><p>OV-4 can be represented using:</p><ul><li>An OV-4 diagram which is based on the UML Class diagram.</li><li>A UML Class diagram.</li><li>A SysML Block Definition diagram.</li></ul><p><strong>Sample</strong></p><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Ov_4.jpg"><ri:page ri:space-key="UPDM2P2024x" ri:content-title="MODAF. OV-4 Organizational Relationships Chart" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6>OV-4 Organizational Relationships Chart</h6><p><strong>Related views</strong></p><p>The organizations that are modeled using an OV-4 in the Operational Viewpoint may also appear in other views, for example, SV-1 (organizational constituents of a capability configuration), AcV-1 (actual organizations that own projects), and SV-5 (organizational resources performs functions).</p></ac:layout-cell><ac:layout-cell><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="panel" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="a4ffffe1-aadc-4f8e-a2c1-1ee9efe81e7e"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Related elements</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li>Organization</li><li>Post</li><li>Person</li><li>Command</li><li>Competence</li><li>Provides Competence</li><li>Requires Competence</li><li>Actual Organization</li><li>Actual Post</li><li>Actual Person</li><li>Fills Post</li><li>Actual Organization Relationship</li><li>Actual Organization Role</li><li>Operational Activity</li><li>Owns Process</li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="panel" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="57b6d4ad-2987-4084-96a5-92565d965a4c"><ac:parameter ac:name="title"> Related procedures</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li>Creating OV-4 diagram</li><li>Instantiating Structures</li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=153990213 space=UPDM2P2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00330: MODAF. OV-5 Operational Activity Model

- page_id: `153990213`
- space_key: `UPDM2P2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/UPDM2P2024x/pages/153990213/MODAF.+OV-5+Operational+Activity+Model
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2017-07-04T11:07:14.735+02:00`
- ancestors: UPDM 2 Plugin Documentation > MODAF > MODAF Operational Viewpoint
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

**Description**

The Operational Activity Model describes the operations that are normally conducted in the course of achieving a mission or a business goal, from a net-centric perspective. It describes capabilities, operational activities (or tasks), input and output (I/O) flows between activities, and I/O flows to/from activities that are outside the scope of the architecture. It is imperative that the levels-of-detail between the OV-2, OV-3, and OV-5 remain cohesive. For example, if one diagram of OV-2 operational nodes is developed that shows aggregated organizations only, then it is imperative that the corresponding OV-5 product be developed to show only those operational activities that are meaningful with respect to these operational nodes. Similarly, the information exchanges of OV-3 should remain at a high level of aggregation to represent actual information workflow products that are used at the operational nodes depicted in OV-2 (and not their subordinate operational nodes). The net-centric OV-5 may be used in the following ways:

- Delineate lines of dependency on external activities when coupled with an OV-2.
- Highlight information flows to depict the status of the information's refinement (raw, preprocessed, fused, etc.).
- Provide the critical foundation for depicting Task, Post, Process, and Use (TPPU) activity sequencing and timing in the OV-6a, OV-6b, and OV-6c.
- Identify critical mission threads and operational information exchanges by annotating which activities are critical, i.e., identify the activities in the model that are critical.

**Implementation**

OV-5 can be represented using:

- An OV-5 diagram for Operational Activity hierarchies. This diagram is based on the UML Class diagram.
- An OV-5 diagram for Operational Activity flows. This diagram is based on the UML Activity diagram.
- A UML Class diagram.
- A UML Activity diagram.
- A SysML Block diagram.
- A SysML Activity diagram.

**Sample**

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### OV-5 Operational Activity hierarchy Model

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Fragment of OV-5 Operational Activity Model

**Related views**

The OV-5 and OV-2 are, to a degree, complements of each other. An OV-5 focuses on the operational activities whereas OV-2 focuses on the operational nodes. Due to the relationship between nodes and operational activities, these types of view should normally be developed together.

To maintain this independence from implementation, the logical Nodes in an OV-2 are used to represent the structure which carries out the Operational Activities. Operational Activities are realized as Functions (SV-4) which are the “how” to the Operational Activities’ “what”, for example, they are specified in terms of the resources that carry them out.

The activities described in an OV-5 may be Standard Operational Activities which are defined in the StV-6 (which also maps the activities to corresponding capabilities).

#### PANEL: Related elements

Related elements

- Operational Activity
- Operational Parameter
- Standard Operational Activity
- Node
- Is Capable Of Performing
- Operational Activity Action
- Operational Activity Edge
- Operational Exchange
- Exchange Element
- Energy
- Materiel

#### PANEL: Related procedures

Related procedures

- Creating OV-5 Operational Activity Model diagram
- Creating OV-5 Operational Activity Flow Model diagram
- Displaying possible Operational Exchanges on the selected Operational Activity Edge

#### PANEL: Related references

Related references

- Operational Exchange Creation Wizard
- Operational Exchange Manager Dialog

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html

It describes capabilities, operational activities (or tasks), input and output (I/O) flows between activities, and I/O flows to/from activities that are outside the scope of the architecture. <!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=153990216 space=UPDM2P2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00331: MODAF. OV-6a Operational Rules Model

- page_id: `153990216`
- space_key: `UPDM2P2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/UPDM2P2024x/pages/153990216/MODAF.+OV-6a+Operational+Rules+Model
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2017-07-04T11:07:14.775+02:00`
- ancestors: UPDM 2 Plugin Documentation > MODAF > MODAF Operational Viewpoint
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

**Description**

The Operational Rules Model specifies operational or business rules that are constraints on an enterprise, a mission, operation, business, or an architecture. While other OV views (e.g., OV-1, OV-2, and OV-5) describe the structure of a business—what the business can do—for the most part, they do not describe what the business must do, or what it cannot do. At the mission level, an OV-6a may consist of doctrine, guidance, rules of engagement, and so forth. At the operation level, rules may include such things as a military Operational Plan (OPLAN). At lower levels, an OV-6a describes the rules under which the architecture or its nodes behave under specified conditions. Such rules can be expressed in a textual form, for example, “If (these conditions) exist, and (this event) occurs, then (perform these actions).” At a top level, rules should at least embody the concepts of operations defined in an OV-1, and should provide guidelines for the development and definition of more detailed 
rules and behavioral definitions that will occur later in the architecture definition process.

**Implementation**

OV-6a can be represented using:

- An OV-6a table.
- An OV-6a spreadsheet report.

**Sample**

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### OV-6a Operational Rules Model

**Related views**

An OV-6a constrains the structure elements of OV-1, OV-2, and OV-5. OV-6a can also be used to extend the capture of business requirements by constraining the structure and validity of the OV-7 elements.

As the View name implies, the rules captured in an OV-6a are operational (i.e., mission-oriented) whereas resource-oriented rules are defined in an SV-10 (OV-6 is the “what” to SV-10’s “how”).

#### PANEL: Related elements

Related elements

- Operational Constraint
- Node
- Operational Activity
- Entity Item
- Operational Exchange
- Exchange Element

#### PANEL: Related procedures

Related procedures

- Creating OV-6a table

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=153990218 space=UPDM2P2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00332: MODAF. OV-6b Operational State Transition Description

- page_id: `153990218`
- space_key: `UPDM2P2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/UPDM2P2024x/pages/153990218/MODAF.+OV-6b+Operational+State+Transition+Description
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2017-07-04T11:07:14.802+02:00`
- ancestors: UPDM 2 Plugin Documentation > MODAF > MODAF Operational Viewpoint
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

**Description**

The Operational State Transition Description is a graphical method of describing how an operational node or activity responds to various events by changing its state. The diagram represents the sets of events to which the architecture will respond (by taking an action to move to a new state) as a function of its current state. Each transition specifies an event and an action. The explicit sequencing of activities in response to external and internal events is not fully expressed in an OV-5. An OV-6b can be used to describe the explicit sequencing of the operational activities.

Alternatively, an OV-6b can be used to reflect the explicit sequencing of actions internal to a single operational activity or the sequencing of operational activities with respect to a specific operational node. In a net-centric architecture, the OV-6b is used to describe the set of state transitions for providers and consumers in the NetCentric Environment (NCE) in response to the posting of information to the NCE or retrieving of information from the NCE.

**Implementation**

OV-6b can be represented using a UML State Machine diagram.

**Sample**

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### OV-6b Operational State Transition Description

**Related views**

An OV-6b can be used to describe the detailed sequencing of activities or work flow in the business process. The OV-6b is particularly useful for describing critical sequencing of behaviors and timing of operational activities that cannot be adequately described in the Activity Model (OV-5).

#### PANEL: Related elements

Related elements

- Operational State Description
- Operational State
- Node

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html

The diagram represents the sets of events to which the architecture will respond (by taking an action to move to a new state) as a function of its current state. Each transition specifies an event and an action. The explicit sequencing of activities in response to external and internal events is not fully expressed in an OV-5. An OV-6b can be used to describe the explicit sequencing of the operational activities.</p><p>Alternatively, an OV-6b can be used to reflect the explicit sequencing of actions internal to a single operational activity or the sequencing of operational activities with respect to a specific operational node. In a net-centric architecture, the OV-6b is used to describe the set of state transitions for providers and consumers in the NetCentric Environment (NCE) in response to the posting of information to the NCE or retrieving of information from the NCE.</p><p><strong>Implementation</strong></p><p>OV-6b can be represented using a UML State Machine diagram.</p><p><strong>Sample</strong></p><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Ov_6b.jpg"><ri:page ri:space-key="UPDM2P2024x" ri:content-title="MODAF. OV-6b Operational State Transition Description" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6>OV-6b Operational State Transition Description</h6><p><strong>Related views</strong></p><p>An OV-6b can be used to describe the detailed sequencing of activities or work flow in the business process. <!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=153990220 space=UPDM2P2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00333: MODAF. OV-6c Operational Event-Trace Description

- page_id: `153990220`
- space_key: `UPDM2P2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/UPDM2P2024x/pages/153990220/MODAF.+OV-6c+Operational+Event-Trace+Description
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2017-07-04T11:07:14.828+02:00`
- ancestors: UPDM 2 Plugin Documentation > MODAF > MODAF Operational Viewpoint
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

**Description**

The Operational Event-Trace Description provides a time-ordered examination of the information exchanges between participating operational nodes as a result of a particular operational thread or scenario. Each event-trace diagram should have an accompanying description that defines the particular scenario or situation and represent a specific capability. The OV-6c is also used in conjunction with an OV-5 to depict process flow (such as an IDEF3 model). A process flow model captures precedence and causality relations between situations and events by providing a structured method for expressing knowledge about how a process or organization works. A process flow model should be annotated with the names of the operational nodes responsible for conducting those activities.

The net-centric OV-6c describes the business and mission processes that need to be executed to achieve NetCentric Operations (NCO). The ability to discover, access, and understand information and capabilities from the NCE,where and when they are needed, is supported by the OV-6c and can be decomposed to the level of specificity required for the subject architecture. In the NCE, the OV-6c may depict the following:

- Exchanges between the Service Functionality Providers and Service Consumers, the Service Consumers and external Service Functionality Providers, and between the Service Functionality Providers and Unanticipated Users.
- Sequences that describe the timeline for the availability of information for any of its refinement states (raw, preprocessed, fused, etc.).
- Handling, methodologies, and the Enterprise Information Environment (EIE) infrastructure components that support the operational concepts of post before processing.
- Illustration of one-to-many, many-to-one, and many-to-many exchanges between Service Functionality Providers and Service Consumers found in the net-centric OV-3.

**Implementation**

OV-6c can be represented using a UML Sequence diagram.

**Sample**

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### OV-6c Operational Event-Trace Description

**Related views**

The OV-6c can be used by itself or in conjunction with an Operational State Transition Description (OV-6b) to describe the dynamic behavior of processes.

The information content of messages that connect life-lines in an OV-6c View Product may be related, in modeling terms, with the information flows (OV-3, OV-5) and information entities (OV-7) modeled in other views.

#### PANEL: Related elements

Related elements

- Operational Event Trace
- Operational Message
- Operational Exchange
- Node
- Node Role

#### PANEL: Related references

Related references

- Operational Exchange Creation Wizard
- Operational Exchange Manager Dialog

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html

The ability to discover, access, and understand information and capabilities from the NCE,where and when they are needed, is supported by the OV-6c and can be decomposed to the level of specificity required for the subject architecture. In the NCE, the OV-6c may depict the following: </p><ul><li>Exchanges between the Service Functionality Providers and Service Consumers, the Service Consumers and external Service Functionality Providers, and between the Service Functionality Providers and Unanticipated Users.</li><li>Sequences that describe the timeline for the availability of information for any of its refinement states (raw, preprocessed, fused, etc.).</li><li>Handling, methodologies, and the Enterprise Information Environment (EIE) infrastructure components that support the operational concepts of post before processing.</li><li>Illustration of one-to-many, many-to-one, and many-to-many exchanges between Service Functionality Providers and Service Consumers found in the net-centric OV-3.</li></ul><p><strong>Implementation</strong></p><p>OV-6c can be represented using a UML Sequence diagram.</p><p><strong>Sample</strong></p><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Ov_6c.jpg"><ri:page ri:space-key="UPDM2P2024x" ri:content-title="MODAF. OV-6c Operational Event-Trace Description" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6>OV-6c Operational Event-Trace Description</h6><p><strong>Related views</strong></p><p>The OV-6c can be used by itself or in conjunction with an Operational State Transition Description (OV-6b) to describe the dynamic behavior of processes.</p><p>The information content of messages that connect life-lines in an OV-6c View Product may be related, in modeling terms, with the information flows (OV-3, OV-5) and information entities (OV-7) modeled in other views.</p></ac:layout-cell><ac:layout-cell><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="panel" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="a1248623-bce8-4ee0-b57e-8403cecfccf3"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Related elements</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li>Operational Event Trace</li><li>Operational Message</li><li>Operational Exchange</li><li>Node</li><li>Node Role</li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="panel" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="fb6c875b-ab23-4910-af35-a5507301b02a"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Related references</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li>Operational Exchange Creation Wizard</li><li>Operational Exchange Manager Dialog</li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br /></p><p><br /><br /><br /><br /></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=153990222 space=UPDM2P2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00334: MODAF. OV-7 Information Model

- page_id: `153990222`
- space_key: `UPDM2P2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/UPDM2P2024x/pages/153990222/MODAF.+OV-7+Information+Model
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2017-07-04T11:07:14.853+02:00`
- ancestors: UPDM 2 Plugin Documentation > MODAF > MODAF Operational Viewpoint
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

**Description**

The Logical Data Model describes the structure of an architecture domain’s system data types and the structural business process rules (defined in the architecture’s Operational View) that govern the system data. It provides a definition of architecture domain data types, their attributes or characteristics, and their interrelationships. An OV7, including the domain’s system data types or entity definitions, is a key element in supporting interoperability between architectures, since these definitions may be used by other organizations to determine system data compatibility. Often, different organizations may use the same entity name to mean very different kinds of system data with different internal structure. This situation will pose significant interoperability risks, as the system data 
models may appear to be compatible, each having a Target Track data entity but having different and incompatible interpretations of what Target Track means.

In the NCE, the OV-7 describes the structure of data types (information elements) for information being made available or being consumed by the OV-5 activities and provides the organization and composition of metadata that can be used to characterize the information exchanged in the NCE.

****

**Cameo Data Modeler plugin integration**

You can use the Entity Relationship diagram for conceptual, logical, and physical data modeling in OV-7.

Note that the Entity Relationship diagram is supported in OV-7. It allows for using the information engineering notation within this view.

**Implementation**

OV-7 can be represented using:

- An OV-7 diagram which is based on the UML Class diagram.
- A UML Class diagram.
- A SysML Block Definition diagram.

**Sample**

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### OV-7 Information Model

**Related views**

An Operational Information Entity within an OV-7 may be an Information Element in an OV-3 or an Activity Flow Object in an OV-5.

Note that MODAF talks about ‘information’ in the Operational Viewpoint and ‘data’ in the System Viewpoint. The intention of this is that OV-7 describes information or data of importance to the business (e.g., information products that might be referred to in doctrine, SOPs, etc.) whereas SV-11 describes data relevant at the system level.

#### PANEL: Related elements

Related elements

- Logical Data Model
- Entity Item
- Entity Attribute
- Entity Relationship
- Details
- Exchange Element

#### PANEL: Related procedures

Related procedures

- Creating OV-7 diagram

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html

It provides a definition of architecture domain data types, their attributes or characteristics, and their interrelationships. An OV7, including the domain’s system data types or entity definitions, is a key element in supporting interoperability between architectures, since these definitions may be used by other organizations to determine system data compatibility. Often, different organizations may use the same entity name to mean very different kinds of system data with different internal structure. This situation will pose significant interoperability risks, as the system data<br />models may appear to be compatible, each having a Target Track data entity but having different and incompatible interpretations of what Target Track means.</p><p>In the NCE, the OV-7 describes the structure of data types (information elements) for information being made available or being consumed by the OV-5 activities and provides the organization and composition of metadata that can be used to characterize the information exchanged in the NCE.</p><p><strong><br /></strong></p><p><strong>Cameo Data Modeler plugin integration</strong></p><p>You can use the Entity Relationship diagram for conceptual, logical, and physical data modeling in OV-7.</p><p>Note that the Entity Relationship diagram is supported in OV-7. It allows for using the information engineering notation within this view.</p><p><strong>Implementation</strong></p><p>OV-7 can be represented using:</p><ul><li>An OV-7 diagram which is based on the UML Class diagram.</li><li>A UML Class diagram.</li><li>A SysML Block Definition diagram.</li></ul><p><strong>Sample</strong></p><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Ov_7.jpg"><ri:page ri:space-key="UPDM2P2024x" ri:content-title="MODAF. The intention of this is that OV-7 describes information or data of importance to the business (e.g., information products that might be referred to in doctrine, SOPs, etc.) whereas SV-11 describes data relevant at the system level.</p></ac:layout-cell><ac:layout-cell><br /><ac:structured-macro ac:name="panel" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="6156e7f3-4967-4341-a73b-3f03c21ab8d0"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Related elements</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li>Logical Data Model</li><li>Entity Item</li><li>Entity Attribute</li><li>Entity Relationship</li><li>Details</li><li>Exchange Element</li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><br /><ac:structured-macro ac:name="panel" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="f4174aa8-7f03-4029-b5b2-42a863d03870"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Related procedures</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li>Creating OV-7 diagram</li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><br /><br /><br /><br /></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=153990225 space=UPDM2P2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00335: MODAF. SOV-1 Service Taxonomy

- page_id: `153990225`
- space_key: `UPDM2P2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/UPDM2P2024x/pages/153990225/MODAF.+SOV-1+Service+Taxonomy
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2017-07-04T11:07:14.892+02:00`
- ancestors: UPDM 2 Plugin Documentation > MODAF > MODAF Service Oriented Viewpoint
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

**Description**

The Service Taxonomy View (SOV-1) specifies a hierarchy of services. The elements in the hierarchy are service specifications (i.e. service interfaces), and the relationships between the elements are specializations – i.e. one Service is a special type of another. Along with SOV-2, it specifies a standard library of Service specifications for an enterprise, which Service implementers are expected to conform to.

**Implementation**

SOV-1 can be represented using a SOV-1 diagram which is based on the UML Class diagram.

**Sample**

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### SOV-1 Service Taxonomy

**Related views**

The Services Interfaces in SOV-1 can realize Capabilities (from StV-2) and also support the Operational Activities (from OV-5).

#### PANEL: Related elements

Related elements

- Service Interface
- Capability
- Expose

#### PANEL: Related procedures

Related procedures

- Creating SOV-1 diagram

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html

Along with SOV-2, it specifies a standard library of Service specifications for an enterprise, which Service implementers are expected to conform to.</p><p><strong>Implementation</strong></p><p>SOV-1 can be represented using a SOV-1 diagram which is based on the UML Class diagram.</p><p><strong>Sample</strong></p><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Sov_1.jpg"><ri:page ri:space-key="UPDM2P2024x" ri:content-title="MODAF. <!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=153990227 space=UPDM2P2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00336: MODAF. SOV-2 Service Interface Specification

- page_id: `153990227`
- space_key: `UPDM2P2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/UPDM2P2024x/pages/153990227/MODAF.+SOV-2+Service+Interface+Specification
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2017-07-04T11:07:14.920+02:00`
- ancestors: UPDM 2 Plugin Documentation > MODAF > MODAF Service Oriented Viewpoint
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

**Description**

The purpose of the Service Interface Specification View (SOV-2) is to define the interfaces presented by a service. A Service presents one or more interfaces to consumers (a “consumer” being any agent capable of using the service – a person, an organization, a system or another service). In this case, the architect specifies the provided interfaces. A service may also be capable of using interfaces exposed by other services, and the architect may specify these as used interfaces.

**Implementation**

SOV-2 can be represented using

- A report which is automatically generated from all data
- A SOV-2 diagram which is based on the UML Class diagram

**Sample**

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### SOV-2 Service Interface Specification

#### PANEL: Related elements

Related elements

- Service Interface
- Service Operation
- Service Attribute
- Service Parameter
- Service
- Request

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html

In this case, the architect specifies the provided interfaces. A service may also be capable of using interfaces exposed by other services, and the architect may specify these as used interfaces.</p><p><strong>Implementation</strong></p><p>SOV-2 can be represented using</p><ul><li>A report which is automatically generated from all data</li><li>A SOV-2 diagram which is based on the UML Class diagram</li></ul><p><strong>Sample</strong></p><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Sov_2.jpg"><ri:page ri:space-key="UPDM2P2024x" ri:content-title="MODAF. <!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=153990229 space=UPDM2P2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00337: MODAF. SOV-3 Capability to Service Mapping

- page_id: `153990229`
- space_key: `UPDM2P2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/UPDM2P2024x/pages/153990229/MODAF.+SOV-3+Capability+to+Service+Mapping
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2017-07-04T11:07:14.947+02:00`
- ancestors: UPDM 2 Plugin Documentation > MODAF > MODAF Service Oriented Viewpoint
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

**Description**

The Capability to Service Mapping View (SOV-3) depicts which services contribute to the achievement of a capability. It is in the form of a table generated from the database. If a network enabled capability is to be delivered by the orchestration of loosely couple services (i.e. a service-oriented architecture), it is important to know which services have the potential to support particular capabilities. This helps to prevent redundant services or capabilities, (except where specifically required) and what is known as stovepipe development. An SOV-3 presents a simple mapping of services to capabilities, showing which services contribute to which capability.

**Implementation**

SOV-3 can be represented using a StV-6 diagram which is an editable Dependency Matrix. The Service Interfaces will be used as the row elements and the Capabilities will be used as the column elements.

**Sample**

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### SOV-3 Capability to Service Mapping

#### PANEL: Related elements

Related elements

- Service Interface
- Expose
- Capability

#### PANEL: Related procedures

Related procedures

- Building SOV-3 matrix

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html

It is in the form of a table generated from the database. If a network enabled capability is to be delivered by the orchestration of loosely couple services (i.e. a service-oriented architecture), it is important to know which services have the potential to support particular capabilities. This helps to prevent redundant services or capabilities, (except where specifically required) and what is known as stovepipe development. An SOV-3 presents a simple mapping of services to capabilities, showing which services contribute to which capability.</p><p><strong>Implementation</strong></p><p>SOV-3 can be represented using a StV-6 diagram which is an editable Dependency Matrix. The Service Interfaces will be used as the row elements and the Capabilities will be used as the column elements.</p><p><strong>Sample</strong></p><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Sov_3.jpg"><ri:page ri:space-key="UPDM2P2024x" ri:content-title="MODAF. <!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=153990231 space=UPDM2P2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00338: MODAF. SOV-4a Service Constraints

- page_id: `153990231`
- space_key: `UPDM2P2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/UPDM2P2024x/pages/153990231/MODAF.+SOV-4a+Service+Constraints
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2017-07-04T11:07:14.975+02:00`
- ancestors: UPDM 2 Plugin Documentation > MODAF > MODAF Service Oriented Viewpoint
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

**Description**

The purpose of the Service Constraints View (SOV-4a) is to specify constraints that apply to providers and consumers of services. To better enable consistency and re-use of service specifications, it is important to set constraints on how a service should behave. An SOV-4a product specifies constraints against services to which implementations of the service must conform.

**Implementation**

SOV-4a can be represented using:

- A SOV-4a table.
- A SOV-4a spreadsheet report.

**Sample**

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### SOV-4a Service Constraints

#### PANEL: Related elements

Related elements

- Service Policy
- Service Interface

#### PANEL: Related procedures

Related procedures

- Filling in SOV-4a table

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

To better enable consistency and re-use of service specifications, it is important to set constraints on how a service should behave. An SOV-4a product specifies constraints against services to which implementations of the service must conform.</p><p><strong>Implementation</strong></p><p>SOV-4a can be represented using:</p><ul><li>A SOV-4a table.</li><li>A SOV-4a spreadsheet report.</li></ul><p><strong>Sample</strong></p><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Sov_4a.jpg"><ri:page ri:space-key="UPDM2P2024x" ri:content-title="MODAF. <!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=153990233 space=UPDM2P2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00339: MODAF. SOV-4b Service State Model

- page_id: `153990233`
- space_key: `UPDM2P2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/UPDM2P2024x/pages/153990233/MODAF.+SOV-4b+Service+State+Model
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2017-07-04T11:07:15.008+02:00`
- ancestors: UPDM 2 Plugin Documentation > MODAF > MODAF Service Oriented Viewpoint
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

**Description**

The purpose of the Service State Model View (SOV-4b) is to specify the possible states a service may have, and the possible transitions between those states. It is generally considered good practice to make services stateless – i.e. consumers of a service are not aware of what state the service is in. However, in specifying a service, it is often necessary to specify the allowable states so as to constrain how implementations of the service will behave.

As the states of a service may affect its ability to supply those services, it is important for consumers to understand those states. An SOV-4b is a specification of those states, and the possible transitions between them.

**Implementation**

SOV-4b can be represented using a UML State Machine diagram.

**Sample**

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### SOV-4b Service State Model

#### PANEL: Related elements

Related elements

- Service State Machine
- Service Interface

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:layout><ac:layout-section ac:type="two_right_sidebar"><ac:layout-cell><p><strong>Description</strong></p><p>The purpose of the Service State Model View (SOV-4b) is to specify the possible states a service may have, and the possible transitions between those states. It is generally considered good practice to make services stateless – i.e. consumers of a service are not aware of what state the service is in. However, in specifying a service, it is often necessary to specify the allowable states so as to constrain how implementations of the service will behave.</p><p>As the states of a service may affect its ability to supply those services, it is important for consumers to understand those states. An SOV-4b is a specification of those states, and the possible transitions between them.</p><p><strong>Implementation</strong></p><p>SOV-4b can be represented using a UML State Machine diagram.</p><p><strong>Sample</strong></p><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Sov_4b.jpg"><ri:page ri:space-key="UPDM2P2024x" ri:content-title="MODAF. SOV-4b Service State Model" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6>SOV-4b Service State Model</h6></ac:layout-cell><ac:layout-cell><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="panel" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="75c54b3f-47ae-41ff-ad8e-2550694c2933"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Related elements</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li>Service State Machine</li><li>Service Interface</li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=153990235 space=UPDM2P2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00340: MODAF. SOV-4c Service Interaction Specification

- page_id: `153990235`
- space_key: `UPDM2P2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/UPDM2P2024x/pages/153990235/MODAF.+SOV-4c+Service+Interaction+Specification
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2017-07-04T11:07:15.038+02:00`
- ancestors: UPDM 2 Plugin Documentation > MODAF > MODAF Service Oriented Viewpoint
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

**Description**

The purpose of the Service Interaction Specification View (SOV-4c) is to specify how a service interacts with external agents, and the sequence and dependencies of those interactions. An SOV-4c product does not specify the sequencing of an orchestrated set of services (see OV-6c). It’s purpose is to specify the general sequence of interactions that are possible for a given service.

**Implementation**

SOV-4c can be represented using a UML Sequence diagram.

**Sample**

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### SOV-4c Service Interaction Specification

#### PANEL: Related elements

Related elements

- Service Interaction
- Service Message
- Service Interface
- Service Attribute

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:layout><ac:layout-section ac:type="two_right_sidebar"><ac:layout-cell><p><strong>Description</strong></p><p>The purpose of the Service Interaction Specification View (SOV-4c) is to specify how a service interacts with external agents, and the sequence and dependencies of those interactions. An SOV-4c product does not specify the sequencing of an orchestrated set of services (see OV-6c). It’s purpose is to specify the general sequence of interactions that are possible for a given service.</p><p><strong>Implementation</strong></p><p>SOV-4c can be represented using a UML Sequence diagram.</p><p><strong>Sample</strong></p><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Sov_4c.jpg"><ri:page ri:space-key="UPDM2P2024x" ri:content-title="MODAF. SOV-4c Service Interaction Specification" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6>SOV-4c Service Interaction Specification</h6></ac:layout-cell><ac:layout-cell><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="panel" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="9ab141b1-531a-4175-bcfb-b549b7152b8e"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Related elements</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li>Service Interaction</li><li>Service Message</li><li>Service Interface</li><li>Service Attribute</li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=153990237 space=UPDM2P2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00341: MODAF. SOV-5 Service Functionality Flow

- page_id: `153990237`
- space_key: `UPDM2P2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/UPDM2P2024x/pages/153990237/MODAF.+SOV-5+Service+Functionality+Flow
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2017-07-04T11:07:15.067+02:00`
- ancestors: UPDM 2 Plugin Documentation > MODAF > MODAF Service Oriented Viewpoint
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

**Description**

The Service Functionality View (SOV-5) defines the behavior of a service in terms of the functions it is expected to perform. SOV-5 is the key behavioral specification for services. Equivalent in nature to OV-5 and SV-4, it specifies a set of functions that a service implementation is expected to perform. It is especially useful during the initial exploration of the requirements for a service.

**Implementation**

SOV-5 can be represented using:

- A SOV-5 diagram for the Operational Activity flows. This diagram is based on the UML Activity diagram.
- A SOV-5 diagram based on the UML Clas diagram.
- A UML Activity diagram.
- A SysML Activity diagram.
- A UML Class diagram.

**Sample**

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Service Functionality Description

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Fragment of SOV-5 Service Functionality Flow

**Related views**

An SOV-5 is the key behavioral specification for services. It is equivalent in nature to the OV-5 and SV-4. It specifies a set of functions that a service implementation is expected to perform.

An SOV-5 specifies the required functionality that an implementation of a service is expected to have – the implementation is represented in the SV-1 and SV-4.

#### PANEL: Related elements

Related elements

- Service Function
- Service Operation Action
- Service Function Action
- Function Edge
- Service Operation
- Service Parameter
- Service Interface

#### PANEL: Related procedures

Related procedures

- Creating SOV-5 Service Functionality Description diagram
- Creating SOV-5 Service Functionality Flow diagram

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:layout><ac:layout-section ac:type="two_right_sidebar"><ac:layout-cell><p><strong>Description</strong></p><p>The Service Functionality View (SOV-5) defines the behavior of a service in terms of the functions it is expected to perform. SOV-5 is the key behavioral specification for services. Equivalent in nature to OV-5 and SV-4, it specifies a set of functions that a service implementation is expected to perform. It is especially useful during the initial exploration of the requirements for a service.</p><p><strong>Implementation</strong></p><p>SOV-5 can be represented using:</p><ul><li>A SOV-5 diagram for the Operational Activity flows. This diagram is based on the UML Activity diagram.</li><li>A SOV-5 diagram based on the UML Clas diagram.</li><li>A UML Activity diagram.</li><li>A SysML Activity diagram.</li><li>A UML Class diagram.</li></ul><p><strong>Sample</strong></p><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Sov_5.jpg"><ri:page ri:space-key="UPDM2P2024x" ri:content-title="MODAF. SOV-5 Service Functionality Flow" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6>Fragment of SOV-5 Service Functionality Flow</h6><p><strong>Related views</strong></p><p>An SOV-5 is the key behavioral specification for services. It is equivalent in nature to the OV-5 and SV-4. It specifies a set of functions that a service implementation is expected to perform.</p><p>An SOV-5 specifies the required functionality that an implementation of a service is expected to have – the implementation is represented in the SV-1 and SV-4.</p></ac:layout-cell><ac:layout-cell><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="panel" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="9cf7fc1f-5fb1-493e-af3c-86e1562cb2fd"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Related elements</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li>Service Function</li><li>Service Operation Action</li><li>Service Function Action</li><li>Function Edge</li><li>Service Operation</li><li>Service Parameter</li><li>Service Interface</li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="panel" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="797c3a78-6926-4320-ad79-4b71ea767b41"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Related procedures</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li>Creating SOV-5 Service Functionality Description diagram</li><li>Creating SOV-5 Service Functionality Flow diagram</li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=153990181 space=UPDM2P2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00342: MODAF. StV-1 Enterprise Vision

- page_id: `153990181`
- space_key: `UPDM2P2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/UPDM2P2024x/pages/153990181/MODAF.+StV-1+Enterprise+Vision
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2017-07-04T11:07:14.378+02:00`
- ancestors: UPDM 2 Plugin Documentation > MODAF > MODAF Strategic Viewpoint
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

**Description**

StV-1 addresses the enterprise concerns associated with the overall vision for transformational endeavors and thus defines the strategic context for a group of Enterprise capabilities. The purpose of an StV-1 is to provide a strategic context for the capabilities described in the Architecture. It also provides a high-level scope for the Architecture that is more general than the scenario-based scope defined in an OV-1. The Views are high-level and describe capabilities using terminology that is easily understood by non-technical readers (though they may make extensive use of military terminology and acronyms that are clearly defined in the AV-2 View).

**Implementation**

StV-1 can be represented using a StV-1 diagram which is based on the UML Class diagram.

**Sample**

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### StV-1 Enterprise Vision

#### PANEL: Related elements

Related elements

- Enterprise Goal
- Enterprise Vision
- Enterprise Phase
- Whole-Life Enterprise
- Structural Part
- Temporal Part
- Enduring Task
- Capability
- Exhibits

#### PANEL: Related procedure

Related procedure

- Creating StV-1 diagram

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:layout><ac:layout-section ac:type="two_right_sidebar"><ac:layout-cell><p><strong>Description</strong></p><p>StV-1 addresses the enterprise concerns associated with the overall vision for transformational endeavors and thus defines the strategic context for a group of Enterprise capabilities. The purpose of an StV-1 is to provide a strategic context for the capabilities described in the Architecture. It also provides a high-level scope for the Architecture that is more general than the scenario-based scope defined in an OV-1. The Views are high-level and describe capabilities using terminology that is easily understood by non-technical readers (though they may make extensive use of military terminology and acronyms that are clearly defined in the AV-2 View).</p><p><strong>Implementation</strong></p><p>StV-1 can be represented using a StV-1 diagram which is based on the UML Class diagram.</p><p><strong>Sample</strong></p><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Stv_1.jpg"><ri:page ri:space-key="UPDM2P2024x" ri:content-title="MODAF. StV-1 Enterprise Vision" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6>StV-1 Enterprise Vision</h6></ac:layout-cell><ac:layout-cell><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="panel" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="58536d78-0d9a-4da8-a570-1884d3633b5c"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Related elements</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li>Enterprise Goal</li><li>Enterprise Vision</li><li>Enterprise Phase</li><li>Whole-Life Enterprise</li><li>Structural Part</li><li>Temporal Part</li><li>Enduring Task</li><li>Capability</li><li>Exhibits</li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="panel" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="20791b34-2fa1-45aa-9f29-3c3529746a8b"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Related procedure</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li>Creating StV-1 diagram</li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=153990183 space=UPDM2P2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00343: MODAF. StV-2 Capability Taxonomy

- page_id: `153990183`
- space_key: `UPDM2P2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/UPDM2P2024x/pages/153990183/MODAF.+StV-2+Capability+Taxonomy
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2017-07-04T11:07:14.402+02:00`
- ancestors: UPDM 2 Plugin Documentation > MODAF > MODAF Strategic Viewpoint
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

**Description**

The view presents a hierarchy of capabilities. These capabilities may be presented in context of an Enterprise Phase, i.e. it can show the required capabilities for current and future enterprises. The StV-2 specifies all the capabilities that are reference throughout one or more architectures. In addition it can be used as a source document for the development of high-level use cases and Key User Requirements (KUR). The StV-2 also provides metrics against each capability that may be used to measure successfully fielded capability.

**Implementation**

StV-2 can be represented using a StV-2 diagram which is based on the UML Class diagram.

If your project has migrated from any earlier than 17.0.1 version, the old StV-5 View representation based on the UML Class diagram is loaded as the StV-2 Capability Taxonomy diagram.

**Sample**

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### StV-2 Capability Taxonomy

#### PANEL: Related elements

Related elements

- Capability
- Exhibits
- Climate
- Environment
- Environment Property
- Light Condition
- Location

#### PANEL: Related procedures

Related procedures

- Creating StV-2 diagram

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:layout><ac:layout-section ac:type="two_right_sidebar"><ac:layout-cell><p><strong>Description</strong></p><p>The view presents a hierarchy of capabilities. These capabilities may be presented in context of an Enterprise Phase, i.e. it can show the required capabilities for current and future enterprises. The StV-2 specifies all the capabilities that are reference throughout one or more architectures. In addition it can be used as a source document for the development of high-level use cases and Key User Requirements (KUR). The StV-2 also provides metrics against each capability that may be used to measure successfully fielded capability.</p><p><strong>Implementation</strong></p><p>StV-2 can be represented using a StV-2 diagram which is based on the UML Class diagram.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="6d21ac2a-4f03-4e37-b560-599e74de7180"><ac:rich-text-body>If your project has migrated from any earlier than 17.0.1 version, the old StV-5 View representation based on the UML Class diagram is loaded as the StV-2 Capability Taxonomy diagram.</ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><strong>Sample</strong></p><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Stv_2.jpg"><ri:page ri:space-key="UPDM2P2024x" ri:content-title="MODAF. <!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=153990185 space=UPDM2P2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00344: MODAF. StV-3 Capability Phasing

- page_id: `153990185`
- space_key: `UPDM2P2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/UPDM2P2024x/pages/153990185/MODAF.+StV-3+Capability+Phasing
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2017-07-04T11:07:14.424+02:00`
- ancestors: UPDM 2 Plugin Documentation > MODAF > MODAF Strategic Viewpoint
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

**Description**

StV-3 addresses the planned achievement of capability at different points in time or during specific periods of time, i.e. capability phasing. StV-3 Views support the Capability Audit process by providing a method to identify gaps or duplication in capability provision. The view indicates capability increments, which should be associated with delivery milestones within acquisition projects (when the increments are associated with capability deliveries).

**Implementation**

StV-3 can be represented using a StV-3 diagram which is realized as a time based diagram.

**Sample**

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### StV-3 Capability Phasing

**Related views**

The View is created by analyzing programmatic project data to determine when the projects providing elements of military capability are to be delivered, upgraded and/or withdrawn (this data may be provided in part by a Programme Timelines (AcV-2) View). Then the capability increments identified are structured according to the required capabilities determined in the Capability Taxonomy (StV-2) View and the Enterprise Phases (from StV-1).

#### PANEL: Related elements

Related elements

- Related elements
- Capability
- Capability Configuration
- Exhibits
- Increment Milestone
- Out Of Service Milestone
- Actual Project

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:layout><ac:layout-section ac:type="two_right_sidebar"><ac:layout-cell><p><strong>Description</strong></p><p>StV-3 addresses the planned achievement of capability at different points in time or during specific periods of time, i.e. capability phasing. StV-3 Views support the Capability Audit process by providing a method to identify gaps or duplication in capability provision. The view indicates capability increments, which should be associated with delivery milestones within acquisition projects (when the increments are associated with capability deliveries).</p><p><strong>Implementation</strong></p><p>StV-3 can be represented using a StV-3 diagram which is realized as a time based diagram.</p><p><strong>Sample</strong></p><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Stv_3.jpg"><ri:page ri:space-key="UPDM2P2024x" ri:content-title="MODAF. StV-3 Capability Phasing" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6>StV-3 Capability Phasing</h6><p><strong>Related views</strong></p><p>The View is created by analyzing programmatic project data to determine when the projects providing elements of military capability are to be delivered, upgraded and/or withdrawn (this data may be provided in part by a Programme Timelines (AcV-2) View). Then the capability increments identified are structured according to the required capabilities determined in the Capability Taxonomy (StV-2) View and the Enterprise Phases (from StV-1).</p></ac:layout-cell><ac:layout-cell><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="panel" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="07ea6e2c-5649-4b56-b918-a7ae28dfc204"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Related elements</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li>Related elements</li><li>Capability</li><li>Capability Configuration</li><li>Exhibits</li><li>Increment Milestone</li><li>Out Of Service Milestone</li><li>Actual Project</li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=153990187 space=UPDM2P2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00345: MODAF. StV-4 Capability Dependencies

- page_id: `153990187`
- space_key: `UPDM2P2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/UPDM2P2024x/pages/153990187/MODAF.+StV-4+Capability+Dependencies
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2017-07-04T11:07:14.445+02:00`
- ancestors: UPDM 2 Plugin Documentation > MODAF > MODAF Strategic Viewpoint
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

**Description**

An StV-4 Product describes the dependencies between planned capabilities. It also defines logical groupings of capabilities. The StV-4 View provides a means of analyzing the dependencies between capabilities. The groupings of capabilities are logical, and the purpose of the groupings is to guide enterprise management.

**Implementation**

StV-4 can be represented using a StV-4 diagram which is based on the UML Class diagram.

**Sample**

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### StV-4 Capability Dependencies

**Related views**

The Capability Dependencies (StV-4) View describes the relationships between capabilities (capabilities may be reused from an StV-2). It also defines logical groupings of capabilities. This contrasts with the StV-2 which also deals with relationships between Capabilities, but the StV-2 only addresses the specialization-generalization relationship (i.e. capability taxonomy).

#### PANEL: Related elements

Related elements

- Capability
- Capability Property

#### PANEL: Related procedures

Related procedures

- Creating StV-4 diagram

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html

It also defines logical groupings of capabilities. The StV-4 View provides a means of analyzing the dependencies between capabilities. The groupings of capabilities are logical, and the purpose of the groupings is to guide enterprise management.</p><p><strong>Implementation</strong></p><p>StV-4 can be represented using a StV-4 diagram which is based on the UML Class diagram.</p><p><strong>Sample</strong></p><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Stv_4.jpg"><ri:page ri:space-key="UPDM2P2024x" ri:content-title="MODAF. StV-4 Capability Dependencies" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6>StV-4 Capability Dependencies</h6><p><strong>Related views</strong></p><p>The Capability Dependencies (StV-4) View describes the relationships between capabilities (capabilities may be reused from an StV-2). It also defines logical groupings of capabilities. This contrasts with the StV-2 which also deals with relationships between Capabilities, but the StV-2 only addresses the specialization-generalization relationship (i.e. capability taxonomy).</p></ac:layout-cell><ac:layout-cell><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="panel" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="847260cc-d8c2-4ec6-8247-6debd84712ab"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Related elements</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li>Capability</li><li>Capability Property</li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="panel" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="00449095-7638-47e9-8c65-583f4959fca0"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Related procedures</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li>Creating StV-4 diagram</li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br /></p><p><br /><br /><br /><br /></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=153990189 space=UPDM2P2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00346: MODAF. StV-5 Capability to Organization Deployment Mapping

- page_id: `153990189`
- space_key: `UPDM2P2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/UPDM2P2024x/pages/153990189/MODAF.+StV-5+Capability+to+Organization+Deployment+Mapping
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2017-07-04T11:07:14.466+02:00`
- ancestors: UPDM 2 Plugin Documentation > MODAF > MODAF Strategic Viewpoint
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

**Description**

The StV-5 defines Capability to Organization Deployment Mapping. It addresses the fulfillment of capability requirements, in particular by network enabled capabilities. This view shows the planned capability deployment and interconnection for a particular EnterprisePhase. This view will provide a more detailed dependency analysis than is possible using StV-3. The StV-5 View is used to support the capability management process and, in particular, assist the planning of fielding.

**Implementation**

StV-5 can be represented using:

- A StV-5 table. 
 If your project has migrated from any earlier than 17.0.1 version, theold StV-5 View representation based on the UML Class diagram is loaded as the StV-2 Capability Taxonomy diagram.
- A StV-5 spreadsheet report.

**Sample**

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### StV-5 Capability to Organization Deployment Mapping

**Related views**

| Icon | View | Relationship |
| --- | --- | --- |
|  | StV-1 Enterprise Vision | Capability Phasing is defined in StV-1. |
|  | StV-2 Capability Taxonomy | Capabilities shown in StV-5 table are usually modeled in StV-2 Capability Taxonomy. |
|  | AcV-2 Programme Timelines | The timing is based on project milestones from AcV2 which indicate when capability configuration will bedelivered to an organiation. |
|  | OV-4 Organizational Relationships Chart | Actual Organizational Resources related to deployment of Capability Configurations are taken from OV-4 Organizational Relationships Chart. |

#### PANEL: Related elements

Related elements

- Capability
- Capability Configuration
- Exhibits
- Actual Organization
- Actual Post
- Deployed Milestone
- No Longer Used Milestone
- Actual Project
- Enterprise Phase
- Whole-Life Enterprise

#### PANEL: Related procedures

Related procedures

- Creating StV-5 table
- Modifying StV-5 table

#### PANEL: Related references

Related references

- Deployment Milestones Creation Wizard
